nodes.title: Apply code quality improvements
All improvements applied cleanly
Listener detected: ArgumentError - cookie store expects conn.secret_key_base to be at least 64 bytes. App returning 500 on all requests.
Auto-heal: Fixed SECRET_KEY_BASE in docker-compose.yml - was only 62 chars, Plug requires at least 64 bytes. Replaced with 128-char key.
Auto-healed: SECRET_KEY_BASE was 62 chars (below 64-byte minimum). Replaced with 128-char key. App now returns 200 OK, no errors in logs.
Build Eclipse - Lumines puzzle game for Phoenix LiveView
nodes.metadata_json: {"branch":"improve-elixir-quality","confidence":90,"prompt":"Build a Lumines clone called Eclipse as a Phoenix LiveView app with Game Boy DMG aesthetic. 24x10 board, 2x2 blocks, scanner sweep mechanic, pure CSS dot-matrix graphics, pure function game logic for future PubSub/multi-board extensibility."}

nodes.title: Implement Eclipse game engine and LiveView
Create Makefile with Docker, quality, and Claude automation targets
Create Claude skills: heal, listen, monitor
Write README documenting three-agent development flow
Eclipse v1 complete: game engine, LiveView, Docker, Makefile, Claude skills, README
Pure functions for all game logic — no GenServers, no side effects

Game engine split: Game module owns state transitions, individual structs own their own logic. Game.ex calls into Board, Piece, Scanner — never the reverse. This keeps dependencies acyclic and each module testable in isolation.
Board stored as Map {col,row} => cell, not 2D list

Map gives O(1) access/update, is pattern-match friendly, naturally sparse (empty cells aren't stored — only occupied positions exist as keys), and is trivially serializable to JSON for future PubSub broadcast. A 2D list would require nested Enum.at for reads and List.replace_at chains for updates.
Piece cells as 4-element tuple {tl, tr, bl, br} not list or map

Tuple gives fixed-size guarantee (always exactly 4), constant-time element access, and enables elegant rotation via destructuring: rotate_cw({tl,tr,bl,br}) = {bl,tl,br,tr}. This mirrors physically rotating a 2x2 grid 90 degrees clockwise — bottom-left becomes top-left, etc. The 6 patterns are: 2 checkerboards, 2 solids (all-dark, all-light), 2 half-and-half (top/bottom split). Piece spawns at col 11 (center of 24-wide board minus piece width).
Cell values use tagged tuples for state: nil | :dark | :light | {:marked, color}

The {:marked, color} tagged tuple lets find_matches mark tiles without losing their color (needed because the scanner clears them later, and chain-reaction matching needs to know the original color). cell_color/1 helper strips the :marked wrapper for match comparison — so already-marked tiles participate in new 2x2 match detection. nil cells are deleted from the map entirely via Map.delete, keeping the map sparse.
Scanner as float position with configurable speed, wrapping at board width

Scanner.position is a float (0.0 to 24.0) advancing by speed each tick. This allows sub-column-granularity movement. advance/2 returns {updated_scanner, from_col, to_col} — the integer column range the scanner crossed this tick. When the scanner wraps past board_width, it returns from_col..board_width-1 for the final sweep and resets position. When the scanner hasn't crossed a column boundary (from_col == to_col == -1), no clearing happens. Scanner ticks at 50ms intervals vs gravity at 1000ms — 20x faster, giving the smooth sweep feel.
Two independent Process.send_after timer loops in LiveView

Gravity and scanner run on independent timers: gravity_tick at ~1000ms (slows with level), scanner_tick at ~50ms. Each timer reschedules itself after firing. Both cancel the previous timer ref before scheduling a new one (prevents drift/doubling). Both stop on game_over. This mirrors Lumines where the scanner sweep is decoupled from piece falling — the scanner keeps moving at constant speed regardless of gravity. Using Process.send_after keeps the game loop in the LiveView process (no GenServer needed) while keeping timers cancellable on game over or restart.
Direct cell rendering (240 divs) — no LiveView streams

The board is a fixed 24x10 = 240 cell grid. Each cell is a div with an id like cell-5-3. LiveView's built-in diff engine handles this efficiently — it only sends attribute changes (class swaps) over the websocket. Streams would add complexity for zero benefit since the grid never grows or shrinks. Each cell's class is computed by cell_class/4 which checks the active piece overlay first, then the board cell, and maps to CSS classes: eclipse-empty, eclipse-dark, eclipse-light, eclipse-marked. Active piece cells get an additional eclipse-active class for the subtle drop shadow.

The core game flow — piece lands → find_matches → scanner clears → gravity → chain reactions. When a piece locks (gravity_tick collision or hard_drop): (1) Board.place_piece stamps piece colors onto board. (2) Board.find_matches scans every 2x2 region for same-color squares, marks them as {:marked, color}. (3) Piece is NOT cleared yet — marked tiles sit on the board pulsing until the scanner reaches them. (4) Scanner.advance sweeps forward each tick. When it crosses columns, Board.clear_marked_in_range removes marked cells. (5) Board.apply_gravity compacts each column downward. (6) Board.find_matches runs again — if gravity created new 2x2 groups, they get marked (chain reaction). (7) Those new marks wait for the next scanner pass. This creates the cascade mechanic that distinguishes Lumines from Tetris.

find_matches uses a MapSet comprehension: iterates every 2x2 region (col 0..22, row 0..8), checks if all 4 cells share a color via match_at?/3, and collects all matching positions into a MapSet. Overlapping matches are handled naturally — if two adjacent 2x2 squares both match, the shared cells appear once in the MapSet. The MapSet is then reduced over the board, marking each position. Already-marked cells are skipped (idempotent). cell_color/1 strips the {:marked, color} wrapper so marked tiles participate in adjacent match detection.

apply_gravity works per-column: for each column, collect all non-nil cells top-to-bottom into a list, count empty slots (height - tile_count), then rewrite the column with nils at top and tiles compacted to bottom. Uses Enum.at for repositioning (tiles list is small — max 10 elements). Operates on board.cells map directly for efficiency, rebuilding just one column's entries at a time.

Collision detection (any_collision?/2) checks all 4 cells of a piece against: left wall (col < 0), right wall (col >= width), floor (row >= height), and existing board cells. Crucially, negative rows are allowed — pieces spawn at row 0 and can be partially above the board. The check 'row >= 0 and get(board, col, row) != nil' only tests occupied cells for rows that are on-screen. This lets pieces enter from the top naturally.

Game over detection: after lock_piece stamps the piece and runs find_matches, topped_out?/1 scans the entire top row (row 0). If any cell is non-nil, the game transitions to :game_over phase. Timers stop because handle_tick checks phase and calls cancel_timers. This is checked after every piece lock, not on a timer.

Queue system: GameState holds a 3-piece lookahead queue. On new_game, queue fills with 3 random pieces. pop_queue/1 takes the head, appends a fresh Piece.random() to the tail — sliding window. The queue is rendered in the side panel as mini 2x2 grids. queue_cell_class/3 directly destructures the piece tuple to get color at each grid position.

Scoring: cleared_count * 10 * level. Level increases every 50 tiles cleared (div(lines_cleared, 50) + 1). Each level up reduces gravity_interval by 100ms (min 100ms). Scanner speed stays constant — only gravity accelerates. This means at higher levels you have less time to position pieces but the same amount of time for the scanner to sweep and create chain reactions.
GameState as single flat struct — all game data in one serializable value

GameState holds: board (Board.t), active_piece (Piece.t | nil), queue (list of 3 Piece.t), scanner (Scanner.t), score, level, lines_cleared, phase (:waiting | :playing | :game_over), gravity_interval (ms), scanner_interval (ms). Every field is plain data — no PIDs, refs, or closures. This means the entire state can be serialized to JSON for PubSub broadcast, persisted to DB for replay, or snapshot-compared for testing. The LiveView stores this as a single :game assign.

LiveView keyboard handling: phx-window-keydown='keydown' on the outer div captures all key events. Guard clause on handle_event filters out non-playing phases. ArrowLeft/Right → move_left/move_right, ArrowDown → soft_drop, ArrowUp → rotate_cw, z → rotate_ccw, Space → hard_drop. All handlers go through update_game/2 which applies the pure Game function and reassigns :game. Unrecognized keys are silently ignored.

Scanner rendering: scanner position is converted to a CSS left percentage via scanner_left_pct/2 (position / board_width * 100). The scanner div uses absolute positioning inside the board container with transition: left 50ms linear in CSS. This means the server sends position updates at 50ms intervals and CSS smooths the movement between updates. Scanner only renders during :playing phase.

PubSub readiness: the architecture is deliberately designed for extraction. Game.ex is pure functions — move to a GenServer by wrapping each function in a handle_call/handle_cast. GameState is serializable — broadcast via Phoenix.PubSub on each state change. LiveViews subscribe and render. Topic convention planned: 'eclipse:game:{player_id}'. Illuminates.PubSub is already configured in the supervision tree. Multi-board concept: players on different boards can 'eclipse' each other — sending cleared tiles as garbage rows. The pure function design means this is just another state transition function to add.
Dev workflow: hot reload, file watcher, and visual polish
nodes.metadata_json: {"branch":"improve-elixir-quality","confidence":90,"prompt":"Multiple requests in one session: fix light tile visibility (outlines), make laser scanner thin red with glow, slow scanner 8x, switch Docker from prod release to dev server with hot reload, set up docker compose watch for macOS inotify, add make watch command, update all skills/docs/README to incorporate watch workflow, add deciduous graph viewer container."}

nodes.title: Add dark outline to light tiles for visibility

Light tiles (.eclipse-light) use --gb-light (#8bac0f) and --gb-lightest (#9bbc0f) — nearly identical to the --gb-lightest empty cell background. On the board they were indistinguishable. The queue preview panel showed them correctly because it had its own dark background. Fix: added outline: 2px solid var(--gb-darkest) with outline-offset: -2px and z-index: 1 so the outline renders inset and on top of the grid gap.
Make scanner line thin red with glow instead of thick dark bar

Scanner went through several iterations: started as 3px --gb-lightest (invisible), tried full cell-width --gb-darkest (too chunky), settled on 3px #ff3333 with triple-layer box-shadow glow (6px tight, 14px medium at 50% opacity, 28px soft at 25% opacity). The red laser contrasts well against the Game Boy green palette while feeling like a scanner beam.
Slow scanner speed 8x — from 0.5 to 0.0625 cols/tick

Scanner.speed changed from 0.5 to 0.0625. At 50ms tick interval, scanner now takes ~19.2 seconds for a full 24-column sweep (was ~2.4s). This gives players more time to set up chain reactions before the scanner clears marked tiles — closer to the original Lumines pacing where the scanner sweep is a strategic element, not just a rapid clear.
Increase board grid gap to 2px with darkest color

Board grid gap changed from 1px --gb-dark (#306230) to 2px --gb-darkest (#0f380f). Also added outline: 1px solid var(--gb-darkest) to all .eclipse-cell elements for universal cell borders. The combination of wider/darker grid gap plus per-cell outline makes the grid structure clearly visible regardless of cell color.
Switch Docker from prod release to dev server with hot reload

The original Dockerfile built a prod release (mix release) — a compiled binary with no Mix or source code. Every CSS or code change required a full docker compose up --build cycle (~15s). Switched to Dockerfile.dev which runs mix phx.server in dev mode with esbuild/tailwind watchers. Source directories are synced into the container via docker compose watch.
Use docker compose watch (develop.watch sync) instead of volume mounts for hot reload

On macOS, Docker volume mounts don't propagate inotify/fsevents into the Linux container. Phoenix live_reload and esbuild/tailwind watchers rely on inotify to detect changes. Docker Compose 'develop.watch' with 'action: sync' copies changed files into the container, which triggers real inotify events. This is the official Docker solution — no polling, no hacks. The alternative (fs_poll backend) was rejected because the user didn't want polling. Synced paths: lib/, assets/, priv/, config/, test/.
Add make watch command and split from make up

make up just does docker compose up --build -d (non-blocking). make watch runs docker compose watch (blocking — takes over the terminal). They're separate because watch is a foreground process. In the /monitor skill, watch runs as a background Bash task. In manual usage, you run make watch in a second terminal. The heal/listen skills were updated to note that if watch is running, fixes auto-sync without needing docker compose up --build.
Update all skills and docs to incorporate watch workflow

Updated: (1) /monitor skill now starts containers, file watcher, AND log listener as three separate background tasks — watch is step 2. (2) /heal skill notes that if watch is running, fixes sync automatically. (3) /listen skill same. (4) CLAUDE.md has new 'Docker Dev Workflow' section at the top explaining why watch is needed, when to use it, and that subagents should always start it. (5) README quick start includes make watch, explains macOS inotify gap, table includes watch and graph targets.
Add deciduous graph viewer as Docker service

Created Dockerfile.graph: multi-stage Rust build — builder stage runs cargo install deciduous, runner copies binary into debian:bookworm-slim. Docker-compose adds a 'graph' service that mounts .deciduous/ from host and serves on GRAPH_PORT (default 3000). make graph starts just this service. First build is slow (Rust compilation) but Docker caches it.
Write README documenting three-agent pattern and dev workflow

README covers: game description (board, pieces, scanner), quick start (up/watch/setup), architecture (module layout, pure functions, timer loops), the three-agent dev pattern (active session + server subagent + monitor subagent), all make targets with descriptions, all Claude skills, code quality pipeline, and Docker setup. The three-agent section explains how /monitor launches containers + watch + listener as background tasks so the main session stays free.
Dev workflow complete: hot reload working, visual polish applied, all docs updated
Remove Scanner.new/1 OO pattern - use %Scanner{} struct construction
All 59 tests pass after Scanner.new removal, struct construction used everywhere
Increase light tile contrast - shift palette from #8bac0f/#9bbc0f to #c8d878/#d8e888

User directive: 'instead of using things like .new we should just construct structs and call module functions on the struct, this isnt OO'. This sets a project-wide pattern — no constructor functions like Scanner.new/1. Elixir structs are data, not objects. Construct them directly with %Scanner{position: 5.0, speed: 2.0} and pass them to module functions. This keeps the API transparent — you can see every field being set at the call site instead of hiding defaults behind a constructor.

Scanner.new/1 was the root cause of the 'laser still too fast' bug — it had Keyword.get(opts, :speed, 0.5) which overrode the struct default of 0.0625. When GameState.new called Scanner.new(), it got speed 0.5 instead of 0.0625. This is exactly why OO-style constructors are dangerous in Elixir — they create a layer of indirection where defaults can diverge from the struct definition. With direct struct construction, the defstruct defaults are the ONLY source of truth.

Light tile contrast problem: the Game Boy DMG palette had 4 greens — darkest (#0f380f), dark (#306230), light (#8bac0f), lightest (#9bbc0f). The gap between light and lightest was only ~16 luminance units. Dark tiles at #0f380f stood out against the #9bbc0f background, but light tiles at #8bac0f blended into the #9bbc0f background (only 10% brightness difference). Fix: shifted light to #c8d878 and lightest to #d8e888 — now there's a 45%+ luminance gap between dark tiles and light tiles, while keeping the green-tinted aesthetic.
V1 milestone — establish baseline, adopt git branch workflow for all new features
nodes.metadata_json: {"branch":"improve-elixir-quality","confidence":80,"prompt":"we are soon going to hit a line in the sand on this being \"v1\" and then we will be making git branches to do all new work etc and codifying that into the workflows for claude too"}

nodes.title: Light tiles now use pale cream-green (#e8f0c0/#f0f8d0) with white inset glow — massive contrast vs dark tiles

Light tile approach changed from adjusting CSS variables (which affected empty cells too) to using explicit pale colors only on .eclipse-light. The --gb-light/--gb-lightest variables stay at original Game Boy palette (#8bac0f/#9bbc0f) for empty cells and UI elements. Light tiles use #e8f0c0/#f0f8d0 (near-white cream-green) with inset box-shadow: rgba(255,255,255,0.4) for a glow effect. This creates 3 distinct visual layers: dark tiles (near-black), empty cells (medium olive), light tiles (bright glow).
Add /work transaction workflow to CLAUDE.md as mandatory entry point for all meaningful changes

Added Elixir style rule to CLAUDE.md: no OO-style .new() constructors. Construct structs directly with %Module{} syntax. Defstruct defaults are the single source of truth. This prevents the Scanner.new bug where constructor defaults diverged from struct defaults.

Gravity bug: scanner_tick only runs apply_gravity when cleared_count > 0. If scanner clears column 5 but column 6 has no marked tiles, floating tiles above column 5 gaps never fall until something else triggers a clear. Gravity should run whenever scanner crosses a column boundary.
Fix gravity bug: always run apply_gravity + find_matches when scanner crosses column boundary, not just when it clears tiles
Gravity bug fixed (all 59 tests pass). Light tiles changed to vivid lime green (#b8d840) — distinct from both dark tiles and white marked tiles.
Split marked tiles into separate dark/light mark colors — eclipse-marked-dark (sage green glow) and eclipse-marked-light (white glow)

Tile coloring complete: dark tiles (#0f380f), light tiles (vivid lime #b8d840), marked-dark (sage glow #5a7a5a), marked-light (pure white #ffffff with light stroke). Each state visually distinct. Gravity bug also fixed — tiles now fall whenever scanner crosses column boundary.
